metadata.title: T-Learner Uplift Model: Who Buys Because of the Ad?
metadata.note: CLI video: B00 intro · B01 PROBLEM · B02 ASK · B03 CODE · B04 OUTPUT(manim) · B05 CHANGE · B06 slate · B07 slate · B08 slate · B09 outro.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged

skin_warnings: B00: palette=claude but the cold open is 'NikBearBrownOpen' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B09: palette=claude but the outro is 'NikBearBrownOutro' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

beats.role_note: the problem, the stakes — BEFORE the CLI
beats.narration_text: Your churn model predicts who will leave. Your revenue model predicts who will spend more. Neither tells you who will respond to a discount because of the discount. Uplift modeling closes that gap.
beats.visual_intent: title card: 'uplift modeling — not prediction, causation'; distinction between likely buyers and persuadable buyers

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Your churn model predicts who will leave. Your revenue model predicts who will spend more. Neither tells you who will respond to a discount because of the disco

beats.narration_text: Can Claude write a T-learner uplift model and show the Qini gain curve?

props.command: claude "Write a Python uplift model: generate 2000 rows with features age/income/prior_purchase, binary treatment T, outcome Y where true CATE varies by segment. Fit T-learner (two LogisticRegression). Compute individual uplift scores. Print Qini gain table (deciles, cumulative uplift, random baseline). No external causal libraries."

beats.narration_text: Read it. The T-learner fits two separate models — one on the treated group, one on control. The CATE estimate is the gap between those two predictions for each individual.

props.code:
# uplift_t_learner.py — Claude Code output

def fit_t_learner(X_treat, y_treat, X_ctrl, y_ctrl):
    m1 = LogisticRegression().fit(X_treat, y_treat)
    m0 = LogisticRegression().fit(X_ctrl, y_ctrl)
    return m1, m0

def predict_cate(m1, m0, X):
    # CATE = E[Y|T=1,X] - E[Y|T=0,X]
    return m1.predict_proba(X)[:,1] - m0.predict_proba(X)[:,1]

# Qini: sort by CATE desc, compute cumulative lift vs random

beats.narration_text: The Qini curve shows: if you target users in order of predicted CATE, the top decile delivers 2.2 times the incremental conversions of a random same-size group.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: Qini curve + per-decile uplift bars

beats.narration_text: The teardown: a high predicted probability of purchase is not the same as a high predicted causal effect. Marketing models confuse the two — and waste budget on users who would have bought anyway.

props.command:
claude "update uplift_t_learner.py:
  -> add 'sleeping dog' detection (high purchase prob, low CATE)
  -> flag users predicted to buy regardless of treatment
  -> recompute Qini excluding sleeping dogs"

beats.narration_text: The updated model now identifies sleeping dogs — users with high purchase probability but near-zero CATE. Removing them from the campaign target list reduces wasted spend without reducing incremental revenue.
beats.visual_intent: slate: sleeping dog detection output; before/after Qini comparison

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The updated model now identifies sleeping dogs — users with high purchase probability but near-zero CATE. Removing them from the campaign target list reduces wa

beats.narration_text: The T-learner separates estimation by treatment status. X-learner and doubly robust learners extend this with propensity weighting — covered in the next episodes.
beats.visual_intent: recap card: T-learner formula CATE = E[Y|T=1,X] - E[Y|T=0,X]; Qini as validation tool

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The T-learner separates estimation by treatment status. X-learner and doubly robust learners extend this with propensity weighting — covered in the next episode

beats.narration_text: Your move: run a T-learner on your next A/B test before it ends. Compute CATE scores. Check your top predicted decile vs random. If the Qini coefficient is near zero, your model is predicting purchase, not uplift.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: run T-learner, compute Qini, compare to random baseline
